title: Czech goulash
subtitle: Bohemian beef and onion stew with caraway and marjoram
excerpt: A thick, savoury beef stew built on a base of slowly cooked onion, sweet paprika, caraway, and marjoram. The Czech version of guláš, saucier and more caraway-forward than the Hungarian, served with bread dumplings to soak up every drop.
sourceNotes: Czech guláš (goulash) shares a common origin with Hungarian gulyás but has evolved into a distinct dish. Where Hungarian goulash is brothier and relies primarily on paprika, Czech guláš is a thick, dense stew in which caraway and marjoram play as important a role as the paprika. The onion is cooked down much further, almost to a paste, before the meat is added, which gives the sauce its characteristic body. Czech guláš is the definitive pub food of the Czech Republic, served in every pivnice (pub) with knedlíky (bread dumplings) and bread.

content.text: The onion is the foundation. Czech guláš uses substantially more onion than most stews (roughly equal weight of onion to beef), and the onion is cooked slowly for 20 minutes until it has completely dissolved into a golden, fragrant paste. The beef braises in this paste, absorbing its sweetness as the sauce thickens. Do not rush the onion stage.

Caraway and marjoram go in with the paprika. These are not background flavours in Czech guláš: they are structural. Without them, the stew tastes like a simplified Hungarian goulash. With them, it is a distinct dish with its own identity.

Warm the {{sunflower-oil}} in a large heavy saucepan over medium heat. Add the {{onion}} and cook, stirring occasionally, for 20 minutes until very soft, golden, and beginning to caramelise. Add the {{garlic}}, {{caraway-seeds}}, and {{marjoram}} and cook for 2 minutes. Remove from the heat and stir in the {{paprika-sweet}} and {{tomato-puree}}. Return to the heat and immediately add 600 ml of water.

Brown the {{beef-shin}} in a separate pan over high heat in batches until coloured, then add to the onion base. Add the {{bay-leaves}}, {{sea-salt-fine}}, and {{black-pepper-ground}}. Bring to a simmer, cover, and cook over low heat for 90 minutes to 2 hours until the beef is completely tender and the sauce is thick. Remove the bay leaves. If the sauce is too thin, simmer uncovered for 10 minutes more. Serve with knedlíky or bread.

items.fix: Remove the lid and simmer over medium heat for 15 to 20 minutes until reduced to a thick, glossy sauce. Czech guláš sauce should coat the back of a spoon heavily
items.cause: Too much water added, or beef releasing a lot of juice
items.symptom: Sauce thin and watery after 90 minutes

items.fix: Always remove the pan from the heat before adding paprika. The residual heat is enough to bloom it; the liquid added immediately after prevents burning
items.cause: Paprika added to hot oil rather than removed from heat first
items.symptom: Paprika burning bitter notes in the stew

items.fix: The onion should be cooked for a full 20 minutes before any other ingredients are added. It should be golden, very soft, and nearly dissolved
items.cause: Not cooked long enough in the initial stage
items.symptom: Onion still textured in the finished stew

content.text: Czech guláš is pub food in the most direct sense: it is the dish that arrives with a tall glass of Pilsner Urquell in every pivnice in Bohemia and Moravia. It is on the menu of almost every Czech restaurant. The version made in Czech homes uses the same recipe, cooked in a heavy pot on a Sunday afternoon and eaten across several days as it improves. The dumplings served alongside are not a side dish but a structural element. They are the vehicle for the sauce, and the sauce is the point of the whole exercise.
